retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Skin (Integument), Epidermis -- Structure and Function -- Epidermis
retrieved_docs.content: Langerhans cells are dendritic cells that act as the skin's first-line cellular immune defenders and are crucial for antigen presentation. Special stains allow visualization of these cells in the stratum spinosum. Langerhans cells are of mesenchymal origin, derived from CD34-positive bone marrow stem cells, and are part of the mononuclear phagocytic system. These cells contain Birbeck granules and tennis racket-shaped cytoplasmic organelles. Langerhans cells express major histocompatibility complex (MHC) I and MHC II molecules, uptake antigens in the skin, and transport them to the lymph nodes. Merkel cells are oval-shaped modified epidermal cells found in the stratum basale, directly above the basement membrane. These cells serve as mechanoreceptors for light touch and are found in the palms, soles, and oral and genital mucosa, with the highest concentration in the fingertips. Merkel cells bind to adjoining keratinocytes through desmosomes and contain intermediate keratin filaments. The cell membranes of Merkel cells interact with free nerve endings in the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: First Degree Burn -- Introduction -- Embryological Origin
retrieved_docs.content: The epidermis arises from surface ectoderm with specialized cells such as antigen-processing Langerhans cells of bone marrow origin, and melanocytes and pressure-sensing Merkel cells from neural crest origin. Blood supply is from diffusion through the dermoepidermal junction. The histology of the epidermis is of a stratified, squamous epithelium that is composed of keratinocytes. The epidermis can further be stratified by layers which are: stratum germinativum, stratum spinosum, stratum granulosum, and stratum corneum. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Epidermis Histology -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: As cells rise through the stratum spinosum, keratinocytes begin to flatten out, and keratohyalin granules become readily apparent, giving the next layer the stratum granulosum. Other granules are present, including the lamellar granules, which contain glucosylceramides and an assortment of lipids and proteins that are eventually used to bond cells together. [1] The stratum lucidum is found more prominently on acral skin or any skin that is irritated and chronically scratched. The outermost layer of the skin is the stratum corneum, where the cells eventually flake off. In normal skin, our epidermis contains four indigenous cell populations: keratinocytes, melanocytes, Langerhans cells, and Merkel cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Skin (Integument), Epidermis -- Structure and Function -- Epidermis
retrieved_docs.content: Cells of the epidermis: The epidermal cells include keratinocytes, melanocytes, and Langerhans and Merkel cells (see Image. Cells of the Epidermis). Keratinocytes are the predominant cells of the epidermis, originating from the basal layer. These cells produce keratin and lipids essential for forming the epidermal water barrier. Keratinocytes also contribute to calcium regulation by enabling UVB light absorption in the skin, which is critical for vitamin D activation. Melanocytes derive from neural crest cells and primarily synthesize melanin, the main skin pigment component. These cells are found between stratum basale cells. UVB light stimulates melanin secretion, protecting against further UV radiation exposure and acting as a built-in sunscreen. Melanin forms during the conversion of tyrosine to dihydroxyphenylalanine by the enzyme tyrosinase. Melanin then travels from cell to cell, relying on the long processes connecting the melanocytes to the neighboring epidermal cells. Melanin granules from melanocytes transit through the lengthy processes to the cytoplasm of basal keratinocytes. This transfer occurs through cytocrine secretion, where keratinocytes phagocytose the tips of melanocyte processes.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Skin -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: Different layers of the epidermis are visible by examining sectioned epidermal tissue under hematoxylin and eosin staining under light microscopy: Stratum Basalis: characterized by cuboidal or low columnar cells with basophilic cytoplasm and melanocytes. Melanocytes present as rounded cell bodies with clear cytoplasm and slender cytoplasmic processes Stratum Spinosum: characterized by desmosomes attaching polyhedral shaped keratinocytes with a round to oval nuclei and prominent nucleoli. Desmosome junctions appear as spines between cells Stratum granulosum: characterized by keratinocytes with dense, ovoid, and basophilic keratohyalin granules Stratum lucidum: characterized by a thin homogenous eosinophilic zone that is difficult to identify in H&E sections Stratum corneum: characterized by keratin in anucleate cells in a basket weave pattern

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Skin (Integument), Epidermis -- Structure and Function -- Epidermis
retrieved_docs.content: Layers of the epidermis: From the deepest to the most superficial, the epidermal layers are the stratum basale, stratum spinosum, stratum granulosum, stratum lucidum, and stratum corneum. The stratum basale,  also known as stratum germinativum, is separated from the dermis by the basement membrane (basal lamina) and attached to it by hemidesmosomes. The cells in this layer are cuboidal to columnar, mitotically active stem cells that constantly produce keratinocytes. This layer also contains melanocytes. The stratum spinosum, comprising 8 to 10 cell layers, is  also called the prickle cell layer. This layer contains irregular, polyhedral cells with cytoplasmic processes, sometimes called spines, that extend outward and contact neighboring cells by desmosomes. Dendritic cells can be found in this layer. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Skin (Integument) -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Embryologically, the epidermis originates from the surface ectoderm. It is infiltrated with pigment-producing cells known as melanocytes, which originate from the neural crest. [3] Other cell types normally present in the epidermis include keratinocytes , antigen processing Langerhans cells, and Merkel cells (tactile receptors that sense pressure changes at the bottom of the epidermis).

retrieved_docs.title: Onychoscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Histology of the Nail Unit
retrieved_docs.content: The nail plate is characterized by a modified stratum corneum. [22] The keratinized structure consists of compact, well-differentiated, flattened, and enucleated keratinocytes. [23] The nail plate matrix has thick stratified squamous epithelium with long rete ridges and the absence of a granular layer; as it approaches the lunula, it becomes abruptly thin. The loss of nuclei of the matrical keratinocytes in the eosinophilic keratogenous zone forms the lamina of the nail plate. The matrix is the sole subungual location of functioning melanocytes, which have a basal and suprabasal distribution and is the location of Langerhans cells and Merkel cells. [24] [25] The matrical dermis contains a thin papillary and thick reticular layer. The hypodermis is composed of adipocytes with loose connective tissue, large vessels, and nerves; the matrical hypodermis is continuous with the proximal nail fold hypodermis, becoming less dense toward the nail bed.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Skin Appendages -- Structure -- Nail Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The nail matrix lacks a granular layer, has a thick stratified squamous epithelium, long rete ridges, and contains melanocytes, epithelial cells, Merkel cells, stem cells, and Langerhans's cells. It is also known as the germinative zone where stem cells divide, migrate, differentiate, and produce keratin for the formation of the nail. At the edge of the lunula, as the epithelium transitions to the nail bed, the epithelium thins.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Epidermis Histology -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Merkel cells are neuroendocrine cells of epidermal origin that are involved in sensation. [6] [7] Functionally, they act as slow-adapting mechanoreceptors. They exist predominantly in the basal layer of the epidermis and can be identified with a cytokeratin 20 stain.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nail -- Structure -- Nail Matrix
retrieved_docs.content: At the edge of the lunula, an abrupt thinning of the epidermis is visible as the epithelium transitions to the nail bed. [10] When forming the nail plate, matrical keratinocytes flatten and lose their nuclei; this occurs in the eosinophilic keratogenous zone. The prekeratogenous zone and basal layer are below the keratogenous zone. [11] The nail unit has a lower concentration of melanocytes than the skin; however, compared to other nail components, the matrix contains the highest concentration of melanocytes at around 217 cells per square millimeter. [12] Melanocytes in the matrix have a basal and suprabasal distribution, occasionally being found as isolated in the prekeratogenous zone. [12] This suprabasal distribution of melanocytes can cause confusion when interpreting the histology in cases of suspected melanoma. Other cells in the matrix include Langerhans cells and Merkel cells. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Epidermis Histology -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Langerhans cells, derived from monocytes, are the principal antigen-presenting cells found in the epidermis and the most prevalent immune cell type alongside CD8+ T cells. [5] Their function is primarily immune surveillance. The ratio of Langerhans cells to keratinocytes in normal skin is roughly 1 Langerhans cell to 53 keratinocytes. Each Langerhans cell has dendrites that weave through the keratinocytes; these dendrites absorb and metabolize antigenic proteins into peptides. These peptides can then be carried to the regional lymph node with Langerhans cells. Langerhans cells stain with S100, CD1a, and Langerin.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Skin Appendages -- Structure -- The hair follicle is further divided histologically into the inner root sheath and outer root sheath .
retrieved_docs.content: The Outer Root Sheath encloses the inner root sheath and is continuous with the epidermis. This layer contains multipotent stem cells, melanocytes, and keratinocytes. The melanocytes are pigment (melanin) producing cells that originate from the neural crest and contribute to the color of hair, and keratinocytes are keratin producing cells. The Inner Root Sheath further divides into the Henle layer, Huxley layer, and the cuticle . This layer only extends up to the level of where the sebaceous gland meets the hair follicle. Henley's layer : outermost layer made of cuboidal cells in direct contact with the outer root sheath Huxley's layer : the second and middle layer made of two rows of flattened cells that contain granular protoplasm Cuticle : the third, innermost layer made of flat overlapping squamous cells that is continuous with the outermost layer of the hair fiber

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Epidermis Histology -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Melanocytes, under normal conditions, are found intermittently studded in the basal layer. Melanocytes synthesize and store melanin in organelles known as melanosomes. Melanocytes transfer melanosomes via dendrites, primarily to keratinocytes. [4] The melanin content in the skin is responsible for variations in pigmentation. Altered distributions of melanocytes or destruction of melanocytes occur in various pathological conditions leading to hypopigmented areas of skin. Melanocytes are found in the basal layer at a ratio of roughly one melanocyte to 10 keratinocytes when evaluating a histological slide in two dimensions or a ratio of 1 melanocyte to roughly 40 keratinocytes in 3D space. Melanocytes have a variety of stains, but some of the most common ones are S100, Mart-1, MITF, and SOX10.

retrieved_docs.title: Waardenburg Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The characteristic histopathological feature of Waardenburg syndrome is the absence or severe reduction of melanocytes, although residual dihydroxyphenylalanine-positive cells may be present. In hypopigmented skin, melanosomes or indeterminate dendritic cells can be observed within keratinocytes. Langerhans cells in the epidermis appear normal. The density of pigment cells is reduced at the margins of depigmented areas, which is often accompanied by nuclear and cytoplasmic abnormalities. Vacuolated cells may display a clear halo surrounded by melanosomes. [31] Histopathological examination of the inner ear at autopsy reveals spiral ganglion atrophy and a reduction in nerve fibers, accompanied by the absence of the organ of Corti. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Skin (Integument), Epidermis -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The epidermis is derived from ectodermal tissue. The dermis and hypodermis are derived from mesodermal tissue from somites. The mesoderm is also responsible for the formation of Langerhans cells. Neural crest cells, responsible for specialized sensory nerve endings and melanocyte formation, migrate into the epidermis during epidermal development. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1343
retrieved_docs.content: Cyklosporin is classified as a dermatologické látky, indicating that it is a dermatological substance used in the treatment of skin conditions. Dermatologické látky constitute a broad category of agents applied to the skin, and within this category keratolytika are identified as a child subclass, comprising substances that promote the shedding of the stratum corneum. Consequently, both cyklosporin and keratolytika belong to the larger group of dermatologické látky, with keratolytika representing a specific functional subgroup. This hierarchical organization aids clinicians in understanding the categorization of skin‑targeted medications and in selecting appropriate therapies based on their mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑epithelial cancer of skin is a broader classification that encompasses the condition described in Arabic as ورم خبيث في الجلد، آخر, indicating an additional malignant tumor of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and its metastatic counterpart illustrate the inverse‑isa relationship between primary and secondary disease. The SMQ also encompasses pancreatic cancer and melanomas of the skin. Malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) serves as the parent concept for melanomas of the skin, which may involve the retina (ret) as a documented finding site, indicating possible ocular involvement. Melanomas of the skin arise from cutaneous melanocytes, whereas malignant lip cancer originates from epithelial cells of the lip; despite an inverse‑isa link in some representations, they are correctly classified as separate aggressive malignancies requiring distinct diagnostic and therapeutic approaches. Neoplasms of uncertain behavior are linked to metastasis, emphasizing the clinical importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: as a pharmacologically active amino acid in certain systemic and injectable products and as a non‑active excipient in a broad range of topical dermatologic and cosmetic preparations, reflecting its versatile role in formulation science.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2729
retrieved_docs.content: Etretinát is classified as a keratolytika, meaning it belongs to the group of keratolytic agents that promote the shedding of the outer keratinized layer of the skin. As a synthetic retinoid, etretinát is used in dermatological practice for conditions that benefit from enhanced epidermal turnover, such as severe psoriasis and other hyperkeratotic skin disorders. Its inclusion in the keratolytika class underscores its mechanism of action in facilitating exfoliation and normalizing skin cell differentiation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1299
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I) are classified as a type of corticosteroid dermatological preparation that is plain, meaning they are weak topical corticosteroid formulations used without additional active ingredients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations, indicating that they represent a highly potent subgroup of topical steroid formulations used in dermatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2690
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of tarsal gland is a specialized form of incision of skin, representing a more specific surgical action that targets the tarsal gland within the eyelid. Because it involves the eyelid structures, this procedure is also classified as one of the other therapeutic procedures on eyelids, conjunctiva, and cornea. Consequently, incision of tarsal gland bridges the general category of skin incisions with the broader group of ocular therapeutic interventions, highlighting its role in both dermatologic and ophthalmologic surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

Understanding these multiple roles assists clinicians and formulators in selecting appropriate parenteral nutrition regimens and designing effective dermatologic therapies.
